cataloging_subject.label: The Testament of Cresseid
cataloging_subject.scope_note: Robert Henryson's late-fifteenth-century Older Scots poem as an intellectual work. The 1593 Charteris print, later editions and excerpts, manuscript witnesses, modern facsimiles, and the U-M/OTA and EEBO-TCP encodings are transmission layers rather than separate works. ‘The Complaint of Cresseid’ is an internal passage, not a second work.

catalog_summary.title: The Testament of Cresseid
catalog_summary.author: Robert Henryson
catalog_summary.editor: No single editor; Charteris 1593 is the source witness and Smith 1908 supplies pagination
catalog_summary.date: Composed in the late fifteenth century; exact date unknown
catalog_summary.region: Scotland; narrative set during the Trojan War

notes: The title is used by every complete early witness according to the METS scholarly introduction; its spelling is normalized here only for catalog display.

titles.value: Robert Henryson's The testament of Cresseid
notes: Discovery title of the U-M/CME electronic item, not an independently attested work title.
The attribution is stable even though Henryson's exact life dates and much of his biography remain uncertain.

date_statements.display: Late fifteenth century; exact date unknown, before Henryson's death around 1500
notes: The value 1480 in the repository XML is a legacy encoding assertion, not adopted here as a secure exact date.

date_statements.display: Recorded in the Asloan manuscript contents, c. 1515–1525; the listed Testament leaves are now missing
notes: This is evidence for an early copy or title, not an extant textual witness and not the date of composition.

date_statements.display: 1532, in William Thynne's edition of Chaucer's works
notes: Thynne preserves the earliest surviving complete text but anglicizes it and prints it anonymously after Troilus and Criseyde.
This describes the work's literary and linguistic context; it does not claim a securely documented place of composition.

language_statements.label: Older Scots
notes: Often described more specifically as Middle Scots. Witnesses and editions may be substantially anglicized; that transmission-layer variation is not assigned to the work as a whole.

form_statements.description: A 616-line poem in 86 stanzas: 79 seven-line rhyme-royal stanzas and seven nine-line stanzas in the Complaint.

assertion.notes: The repository XML also encodes a final ungrouped ‘Finis’ as L N=617; it is a terminator, not a 617th verse line.

genre_statements.label: Tragedy
notes: The narrator calls the work a tragedy.

genre_statements.label: Chaucerian continuation
notes: The label places the poem in the reception tradition of Chaucer's Troilus and Criseyde; Henryson supplies an independent alternative ending rather than a textual continuation or version of Chaucer's poem.

genre_statements.label: Complaint
notes: Restricted to lines 407–469, the seven nine-line stanzas spoken by Cresseid; the XML's second DIV1 heading extends beyond the complaint through the closing narrative.

summaries.value: After invoking Chaucer and another account of Cresseid, the narrator tells how she is abandoned by Diomede, denounces the gods, and is judged in her dream by a planetary parliament with leprosy and exclusion. At the leper hospital she laments her fall in a seven-stanza complaint; afterward, while begging, she encounters Troilus without either recognizing the other. Once told his identity, she makes a testament to the lepers and dies; Troilus receives her ring and erects her tomb. Henryson transforms the unresolved fate of Chaucer's Criseyde into a Scots tragedy about mutability, social judgment, repentance, compassion, and literary authority.

This record describes Henryson's poem, not the 1593 Charteris imprint reproduced in the XML heading, Smith's 1908 editorial presentation and pagination, or the U-M/OTA encoding. The XML's supplied division label ‘The complaint of cresseid’ covers lines 407–617, but the complaint proper occupies only lines 407–469; lines 470–616 return to third-person narrative.

extent.description: One poem modeled in three narrative units: 58 seven-line stanzas before the Complaint, seven nine-line Complaint stanzas, and 21 seven-line closing stanzas. The repository XML contains 86 LG elements and 617 L elements because an ungrouped final ‘Finis’ is encoded as L N=617.

content_parts.label: Narrative before Cresseid's complaint
content_parts.summary: The narrator invokes Chaucer, recounts Cresseid's abandonment and return to her father, and narrates her dream of the planetary parliament, judgment, disease, exclusion, and removal to the leper hospital.

content_parts.label: The Complaint of Cresseid
content_parts.summary: Cresseid reviews her former status, acknowledges her fall, laments her present exclusion, and warns other lovers.

content_parts.label: Closing narrative, testament, and death
content_parts.summary: After the complaint, Cresseid goes begging, encounters Troilus without mutual recognition, learns the almsgiving knight was Troilus, makes her testament, dies, and receives a tomb whose epitaph records her fall.

related_works.title: Troilus and Criseyde
notes: Henryson explicitly invokes and questions Chaucer's account, reuses its characters and predicament, and imagines Cresseid's later fate. The relationship is modeled as literary response and alternative ending, not as a textual version of Chaucer's poem.

evidence.summary: Supplies the electronic title, Henryson attribution, OTA identifier, 1993 encoding date, 1593 Charteris source statement, Smith pagination statement, omissions, headings, and full text.

evidence.citation: Element and stanza-pattern audit of repository HenCres.xml.
evidence.locator: DIV1/DIV2/LG/L/PB elements and line N attributes
evidence.summary: Counts two DIV1, six DIV2, 86 LG, 617 L, and 23 PB elements (Smith pages 2–24). Seventy-nine LG contain seven lines and seven contain nine; 616 verse lines are grouped, while final ‘Finis’ is an ungrouped L misnumbered 617. Lines 407–469 form the seven-stanza Complaint, and lines 470–616 return to closing narrative.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, ‘Robert Henryson's The testament of Cresseid,’ CME item HenCres.
evidence.summary: Confirms the item title, Robert Henryson attribution, 1593 Charteris imprint, OTA U-243-A identifier, public digital-text access, and U-M's current No Copyright–United States statement.

evidence.citation: Middle English Texts Series, ‘The Complete Works of Robert Henryson: General Introduction.’
evidence.summary: Reviews Henryson's uncertain biography, association with Dunfermline, Older Scots language, and probable death by about 1500 without treating conjectural details as certain; its source survey distinguishes the lost Asloan copy, complete prints, fragmentary witnesses, and later Speght-derived manuscript copies.

evidence.citation: Middle English Texts Series, ‘The Testament of Cresseid: Introduction.’
evidence.summary: Provides the work's literary interpretation, Older Scots context, stanza form, title evidence, relation to Chaucer, late-fifteenth-century dating, textual variants, and Charteris's status as the best surviving early witness.

evidence.citation: General Corpus lineage manifest evidence for William Thynne's 1532 Chaucer edition.
evidence.summary: Supports the bibliographic identity of the earliest surviving complete printed text and its original folio location.

evidence.citation: General Corpus lineage manifest evidence for G. Gregory Smith's 1908 edition and direct XML comparison.
evidence.summary: Supports the conclusion that HenCres PB milestones reproduce Smith's pages 2–24 while the XML does not simply reproduce all of Smith's editorial readings.

open_questions.question: Can the poem's composition be dated or localized more narrowly than late-fifteenth-century Scotland without treating literary inference or Henryson's uncertain biography as documentary fact?
next_steps: Consult current Henryson chronology and textual scholarship before adding normalized composition bounds or a precise place.

open_questions.question: Did a now-lost early-sixteenth-century Scottish print transmit the Testament before or independently of Thynne 1532?
next_steps: Review the inventory and bibliographical evidence cited in the METS introduction; do not create a confirmed witness entity from a hypothetical lost print.

notes: The stanza and line counts describe the intellectual poem and are reconciled explicitly with the repository encoding's extra L element for ‘Finis’.

Witness dialect, scribal spelling, Charteris's compositorial and editorial interventions, Smith's emendations, and digital encoding practices remain in the lineage manifest rather than being collapsed into work language or authorship.
The six supplied line-range DIV2 headings in HenCres.xml are encoding navigation, not treated as a stable authorial section hierarchy.
